title: Working with the part that believes showing emotion is weakness
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part that keeps you armored

I know this part. I've sat with it for decades. It's the one that tightens your jaw when your eyes start to sting. The one that straightens your spine when your voice wants to crack. The one that whispers, "Don't let them see. Don't let them know." And you listened. Of course you did. Because somewhere along the line, showing emotion got dangerous. Maybe you were punished for crying as a child. Maybe you were told to "toughen up" or "stop being dramatic." Maybe you watched your parents bury their own feelings so deep that the house felt like a mausoleum of unspoken pain. Here's the thing: that part of you isn't wrong. It's not broken. It's not weak. It's a protector. And it has kept you alive in the only way it knew how.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how our nervous system stores trauma in the body. The part that blocks emotion isn't a character flaw. It's a survival strategy. When you were small and powerless, showing emotion might have made things worse. Maybe a caregiver dismissed your tears, or worse, used them against you. So your nervous system learned: emotion equals danger. Stay flat. Stay safe. Stay armored. That armor got you through. But now you're reading this, and something feels off. Maybe you sense that the armor is also a prison. Maybe you're tired of being untouchable. Maybe you want to feel something, anything, without the part of you that screams "weakness" shutting it all down.

Stay with me here. This part of you is not your enemy. It's a terrified child who learned that vulnerability gets you hurt. And it's doing its job. But its job description was written a long time ago. You get to rewrite it now.

Where this belief comes from

Let's get specific about the wiring. The belief that showing emotion is weakness doesn't come from nowhere. It's learned. And it's learned in relationship. Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems (IFS), would say this part is a "manager" part. It manages your internal world to keep you safe from external threats. And it learned its strategy from your early environment. Think about it. Who taught you that feelings were bad? Was it a parent who couldn't handle your sadness? A sibling who mocked your tears? A culture that valorizes stoicism and calls sensitivity a liability?

I grew up in a house where crying was met with, "I'll give you something to cry about." So I learned to swallow my tears. I learned to smile when I was breaking inside. I learned that the only acceptable emotion was no emotion. And that worked, for a while. But it also meant I couldn't feel joy without guilt. I couldn't ask for help without shame. I couldn't be close to anyone without waiting for the other shoe to drop. The part that believed emotion was weakness kept me isolated, even when I was surrounded by people.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown that trauma literally changes the brain. The amygdala becomes hypervigilant. The prefrontal cortex, the part that helps us regulate emotion, goes offline. So that part of you that blocks emotion isn't just a belief. It's a biological response. Your nervous system is trying to protect you from being overwhelmed. And it's good at its job. Too good, sometimes. Because the cost of that protection is numbness. It's disconnection. It's a life lived half-alive.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps you understand why you learned to hide your feelings in the first place. And understanding is the first step toward something different.

How to work with this part, not against it

Here's where most approaches get it wrong. The wellness industry will tell you to "just feel your feelings" or "let it all out." But that's like telling someone with a broken leg to just walk it off. It doesn't work. And it can actually make things worse. Because that protective part of you isn't going to stand down just because someone on Instagram told you to " vulnerability." It needs to be seen. It needs to be heard. It needs to know that you, the adult you, are now in charge.

So how do you do that? You start by noticing. Not judging. Not fixing. Just noticing. When you feel that urge to shut down, to swallow the tears, to stiffen your body, pause. Say to yourself, "I notice a part of me that doesn't want to feel this right now." That's it. That's the first move. You're not trying to change anything. You're just acknowledging that this part exists. And acknowledgment, believe it or not, is a form of love.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who works extensively with parts, suggests that we thank our protectors. Thank the part that keeps you armored. Thank it for its vigilance. Thank it for getting you through. You don't have to agree with its methods. But you can appreciate its intent. That alone can soften the grip. Wild, right? The part that's been fighting you for years just wanted to be appreciated.

Then, you can start to negotiate. You can say, "I know you're trying to protect me. And I appreciate that. But I'm not a child anymore. I have resources now. I have a nervous system that can handle more than I used to think. Can we try something small together?" Maybe that something small is letting yourself cry for thirty seconds. Maybe it's telling one safe person, "I'm scared to show you this, but I'm going to try." Maybe it's just sitting with the sensation of emotion in your body without trying to push it away.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It walks through how emotional neglect shapes these protective parts and how to start reconnecting with your emotional self.

The nervous system shift that makes change possible

Here's the thing about working with parts: it's not just a mental exercise. It's a body practice. Your nervous system has to feel safe enough to let emotion move through. And that takes time. It takes repetition. It takes small doses of discomfort that don't tip you into overwhelm. Stephen Porges, the researcher behind Polyvagal Theory, calls this "the window of tolerance." It's the zone where you can feel emotion without being flooded or shut down. Your job is to stay in that window, even if it's just for a few breaths at a time.

One way to widen that window is to pair emotion with safety. When a feeling starts to rise, put your hand on your heart. Take a slow breath. Remind yourself, "I'm here now. I'm safe. This feeling is old, and it can pass through me." This is not bypassing. This is not toxic positivity. This is regulation. It's giving your nervous system the message that emotion is not a threat. It's just information. And information can be processed.

Another approach is to work with a structured guide. A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you step-by-step exercises for working with protective parts, including the one that blocks emotion. It's not about forcing change. It's about creating conditions for change to happen naturally.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. It puts the whole experience in context and helps you understand why your parts developed the way they did. Knowledge, in this case, is a form of compassion.

What happens when the armor starts to crack

I want to be honest with you about something. When you start working with the part that believes emotion is weakness, things get messy. You might cry in the grocery store. You might get angry at someone who doesn't deserve it. You might feel grief so old and so deep that it surprises you. That's normal. That's the armor cracking. And it's not a sign that you're doing it wrong. It's a sign that you're doing it right.

But here's the hard part: the world isn't always ready for your emotion. Some people will be uncomfortable. Some will tell you to get back in your box. Some will try to fix you or dismiss you. That's not your problem. That's their unhealed parts reacting to your healing. You get to keep going anyway. You get to let that part of you know, "I see your fear. I hear your warning. And I'm still going to try. Because I deserve to feel. I deserve to be whole."

I won't tell you that the armor will disappear completely. I don't think it ever fully does. But it can get thinner. More flexible. More like a coat you can take off when you're in a safe room, rather than a suit of steel you can never remove. That's the goal. Not perfection. Just a little more access to yourself. A little more permission to be human.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did something tighten? Did something soften? That's your part responding. It's listening. It's watching. And it's learning, slowly, that maybe showing emotion isn't weakness. Maybe it's the bravest thing you can do.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Running on Empty (paid link) - For understanding emotional neglect and its impact on your emotional self.
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook with practical exercises for working with protective parts.
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Validates childhood experiences and helps you understand why you learned to hide feelings.
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - A foundational text that puts trauma recovery in context and supports self-compassion.
